<!DOCTYPE PubmedArticleSet PUBLIC "-//NLM//DTD PubMedArticle, 1st January 2025//EN" "https://dtd.nlm.nih.gov/ncbi/pubmed/out/pubmed_250101.dtd">
<PubmedArticleSet>
<PubmedArticle><MedlineCitation Status="Publisher" Owner="NLM"><PMID Version="1">42440276</PMID><DateRevised><Year>2026</Year><Month>07</Month><Day>13</Day></DateRevised><Article PubModel="Print-Electronic"><Journal><ISSN IssnType="Electronic">1753-4887</ISSN><JournalIssue CitedMedium="Internet"><PubDate><Year>2026</Year><Month>Jul</Month><Day>13</Day></PubDate></JournalIssue><Title>Nutrition reviews</Title><ISOAbbreviation>Nutr Rev</ISOAbbreviation></Journal><ArticleTitle>Duration-Dependent Changes in Body Composition During Prolonged Fasting: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.</ArticleTitle><ELocationID EIdType="pii" ValidYN="Y">nuag092</ELocationID><ELocationID EIdType="doi" ValidYN="Y">10.1093/nutrit/nuag092</ELocationID><Abstract><AbstractText Label="CONTEXT" NlmCategory="BACKGROUND">Prolonged fasting has gained increasing attention as a non-pharmacological strategy for weight reduction and metabolic health improvement. However, the magnitude and composition of fasting-induced weight loss remain unclear, particularly regarding the relative contributions of fat mass, fat-free mass, and total body water, as well as the role of fasting duration.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="OBJECTIVE" NlmCategory="OBJECTIVE">A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to quantify the effects of prolonged fasting on body composition outcomes.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="METHODS" NlmCategory="METHODS">The PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus databases were systematically searched for eligible studies reporting pre-post changes in body composition. Standardized mean changes were calculated as Hedges' g and pooled using random-effects models. Duration effects were evaluated using subgroup analyses (≤3 days vs >3 days) and meta-regression with fasting duration as a continuous moderator. Subgroup analyses were also conducted according to baseline weight status (obese vs. healthy/normal weight) and body composition assessment method.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESULTS" NlmCategory="RESULTS">A total of 49 studies met the inclusion criteria, contributing 150 independent effect sizes across eligible outcomes. Prolonged fasting produced significant reductions in body weight (g = -0.38) and BMI (g = -0.42). Decreases were also observed in fat mass (g = -0.25), fat-free mass (g = -0.51), total body water (g = -0.68), and waist circumference (g = -0.55). Subgroup analyses indicated that body weight reductions were greater in obese participants than in healthy individuals and were more pronounced when fasting duration exceeded 3 days. Meta-regression analyses did not reveal significant duration-response relationships for BMI, fat mass, or fat-free mass, although a non-significant trend toward greater body weight reduction with longer fasting duration was observed.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="CONCLUSIONS" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">Prolonged fasting reduces body weight, BMI, fat mass, and waist circumference but is also associated with substantial losses in fat-free mass and total body water, indicating that fasting-induced weight loss reflects complex changes in body composition rather than isolated fat loss. No clear duration-dependent effects were observed for most outcomes.</AbstractText><CopyrightInformation>© The Author(s) 2026.
